metadata.name: cross_domain
metadata.description: Dialogue that discusses neural network training for 10 turns, then introduces biological evolution as 'a completely different topic.' The two domains share deep structural equivalences never made explicit.

conversation: How does a neural network actually learn?
It makes predictions, measures how wrong they are, then adjusts its weights to be less wrong next time.
The error signal. The gradient tells each weight which direction to move to reduce the mistake.
So it's just minimizing errors repeatedly?
Exactly. Each iteration the network gets slightly better at the task it's being trained on.
What if the adjustments are too large?
It overshoots. The network becomes unstable, bouncing around without settling on a solution.
It converges too slowly, or gets stuck in a local minimum and stops improving.
Completely different topic — how does evolution work?
Organisms vary randomly. Those better suited to their environment survive and reproduce more.
Mutation mostly. Random changes to the genome that get passed to offspring.
Yes. But occasionally one improves survival. That variant spreads through the population.
What determines if a variant spreads?
Selection pressure. How harsh the environment is, how much the trait helps, how fast generations turn over.
So the environment is doing the selecting?
The environment defines what counts as fit. Fitness has no meaning outside that context.
What happens when the environment changes?
The population has to adapt or die. Traits that were advantageous can become liabilities.
Is there such a thing as evolving too fast?
Yes — too much mutation destroys the information accumulated by previous generations.
The population can't adapt to environmental shifts. It gets locked into solutions that no longer fit.
Most deep patterns do, once you see them.

questions.text: What controls the size of each weight adjustment in neural network training?
What happens when a population cannot adapt fast enough to environmental change?
What do learning rate and mutation rate have in common?
questions.description: Synthesis — requires connecting ML and evolution domains

questions.text: How does overfitting in neural networks relate to evolutionary traps?
Why might a neural network and a species both get permanently stuck?
If the training environment defines the task and the environment defines fitness, what is the evolutionary equivalent of a loss function?
Is evolution a kind of learning? Make the case.
questions.description: Cross-domain synthesis — the core aha moment question

questions.text: What concept plays the same structural role in both topics we discussed?
questions.description: Cross-domain primitive — tests whether environment was found
